title: What to Do If Your College Major Project Partner Deserts the Group
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You have been working on a major project worth a significant portion of your final grade. It is a partnership or small group assignment. The deadline is approaching. Then your partner stops responding. Maybe they dropped the class without telling you. Maybe they are still enrolled but have simply disappeared. Either way, their portion of the work is incomplete, and you are staring at a shared file directory with half the content it needs. This is different from a group member who ghosts the chat. This is a structural failure where the project itself is at risk. Here is how to handle it.

Verify What Actually Happened

Before you escalate, confirm the situation. Check whether your partner is still enrolled in the course. Many learning management systems show the class roster. If the partner has officially dropped, their name may have disappeared from the roster. If they are still listed, they are still technically in the class. Try reaching them one more time. Send a brief, direct message: "Hi, I am checking in on the project. The deadline is Friday and I need to know whether you are still working on your section. Please let me know by tonight either way. If I do not hear from you, I will need to talk to the professor about how to proceed."

This message does three things. It communicates urgency. It gives a clear deadline for a response. And it signals that you will escalate if necessary. You are not threatening. You are informing them of your next step. That is fair and professional.

Contact the Instructor Immediately

Do not wait until the deadline. Do not wait to see if your partner reappears. The moment you know the project is at risk, email your instructor. A message sent a week before the deadline gets a different response than a message sent the night before.

Your email should be factual, not emotional. State what happened. Your partner has become unresponsive or has dropped the course. You have attempted to contact them. Their portion of the work is incomplete. You are asking for guidance on how to proceed. Attach evidence if you have it. Screenshots of unanswered messages. The shared file directory showing only your contributions.

Try this: "Dear Professor Martinez, I am writing about the final project for your Biology 220 course. My partner, Alex Chen, has not responded to messages since March 15. I have attempted to reach them three times. I have just checked the course roster and it appears they may have dropped the class. Our project is due April 5, and Alex's assigned sections are incomplete. I am prepared to complete the project on my own, but I wanted to ask how you would like me to handle the division of work and whether any adjustments to the project scope are appropriate given the circumstances."

This email demonstrates that you are proactive, responsible, and focused on solutions. You are not asking the instructor to solve your problem. You are asking for guidance while making it clear you intend to complete the work.

Document Your Solo Work

If the instructor gives you the go-ahead to complete the project alone, document everything. Save every version of the project file. Keep a log of the hours you spend. Note which sections were originally assigned to your partner and which you completed yourself. If the project involves a presentation, note which slides or sections you prepared alone.

This documentation serves two purposes. It protects you if your partner resurfaces at the last minute and tries to claim credit for work they did not do. And it gives your instructor a clear picture of what you accomplished under difficult circumstances. When grading time comes, your instructor will know the project was completed by one person, not two, and can adjust expectations accordingly.

If the Partner Reappears at the Last Minute

Sometimes a missing partner resurfaces a day or two before the deadline with a flurry of messages and hastily completed work. You now face a choice. Accept their contributions and integrate them into the project, or tell them it is too late.

If their work is usable and you have time to integrate it, accept it. The project will be stronger with two people's contributions. If their work is poor quality and integrating it would weaken the project you have built alone, or if it arrives so late that you cannot review it properly, you are within your rights to decline it. Inform your instructor of the situation and ask how to proceed. The instructor may tell you to include the work or may allow you to submit what you completed alone.

What Not to Do

Do not put your partner's name on the project if they did not contribute. This feels like the nice thing to do, especially if you know they were struggling. It is academic dishonesty. If the instructor discovers the partner did no work, both of you can face consequences. Protect yourself.

Do not trash your partner to the instructor. Even if your anger is justified, venting makes you look unprofessional. Stick to the facts. The instructor will draw their own conclusions.

Do not assume the instructor already knows. If your partner dropped the class, the instructor may not have connected that to your project. It is your responsibility to communicate the situation.

The Bottom Line

A partner who deserts a major project is one of the most stressful academic situations you can face. Act quickly. Verify the situation. Contact your instructor before the deadline with a factual, solution-oriented message. Document everything you do alone. If the partner reappears, evaluate their work honestly. Do not put their name on work they did not do. The skills you develop handling this situation, clear communication under pressure, documentation, and self-advocacy, will serve you long after the project is graded and forgotten.
